title: Weekly News: Ad-Tech Drama and Amazon's Power Move
author: The Premium Team
content:
Premium
Print
Weekly News: Ad-Tech Drama and Amazon's Power Move

The Trade Desk is testing 52-week lows, while Amazon just declared war on the logistics industry.

The Premium Team

6:00 AM

Comments
8.0

Catch up quickly on what happened in the market this week.

The Markets This Week:
S&P 500 7,399 (+2.36%) • Nasdaq 26,247 (+4.52%)
Dow 49,609 (+0.22%) • Bitcoin $80,158 (+0.18%)

1. The Trade Desk Faces a Credibility Test

The Trade Desk (NASDAQ: TTD) shares tumbled more than 10% on Friday morning, hitting a multiyear low as the market digests a mixed Q1 report. While the company beat on revenue, a slight earnings miss and conservative guidance have taken a backseat to a brewing industry battle with advertising giant Publicis Groupe (OTC: PUBGY). TTD shares were down 4.9% for the week.

The Publicis standoff: Publicis has reportedly advised its massive roster of global clients against using TTD, alleging issues with fee transparency. While rival agency Omnicom Group (NYSE: OMC) conducted an audit and found no such problems, the mere existence of the dispute is fueling fears of a broader agency pushback.
"The near-term is genuinely cloudy." Sanmeet Deo notes that while the "open internet" thesis remains strong, the market trades on uncertainty. "Publicis manages enormous ad budgets. If their advisory sticks, the revenue impact could be material, and TTD's guidance might not yet reflect that risk."
2. AMD Joins the AI Elites

Results from Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ: AMD) made it clear that Nvidia (NASDAQ: NVDA) is hardly the only beneficiary of the artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure build-out. AMD grew revenue by 38% and earnings per share by 43%, posting a 256% gain in free cash flow. Leading the way was AMD's Data Center segment, which saw revenue grow by 57%. Investors cheered the results, sending AMD shares up by 26% for the week.

"A clear step-up in our growth trajectory with accelerating momentum across the business." During the post-earnings conference call, AMD CEO Lisa Su said strong demand for AI infrastructure should drive gains into the foreseeable future, setting AMD up for continued growth from here.
"Don't underestimate Lisa Su!" This wasn't just a great quarter. For Emily Flippen, it was strong enough to prompt investors to rethink this business. AMD has always been perceived as second-fiddle to Nvidia's graphics processing unit (GPU) dominance, but Flippen says the quarter shows that AMD's central processing unit (CPU) lead can be just as beneficial as Nvidia's GPU stronghold.
3. GameStop Hits Buy Button on eBay

GameStop (NYSE: GME) CEO Ryan Cohen made good on his promise to grow via acquisition, going public with an unsolicited $56 billion offer to acquire the much larger eBay (NASDAQ: EBAY). Cohen said he is considering taking the offer directly to eBay shareholders if the company rejects the bid, saying the combination "could be a legit competitor to Amazon" (NASDAQ: AMZN) under his leadership. Shares of eBay were up 3.4% for the week, while GameStop shares were down 8.5%.

"A Bad Idea for eBay Shareholders": Matt Argersinger is skeptical, questioning the debt GameStop would have to take on and noting that "eBay is arguably in its best shape in years."
Burry backs out: Famed Big Short investor Michael Burry is also not a fan of the proposal, announcing he sold his entire GameStop position. "Any which way I sliced it, the Instant Berkshire thesis was never compatible," Burry said, questioning Cohen's plan.
4. Prime Pain: Amazon Moves Into Logistics

Amazon has a long history of successfully disrupting incumbents. This week, the tech giant turned its attention toward logistics, announcing plans to open up its massive supply chain and delivery networks to third-party customers. The news sent logistics stocks into a tailspin, with United Parcel Service (NYSE: UPS), FedEx (NYSE: FDX), and GXO Logistics (NYSE: GXO) down 6.3%, 3.8%, and 8.1%, respectively, for the week.

What it means for UPS: Matt Argersinger said the move is "particularly damaging" for UPS because the company is already dealing with Amazon shipping volume declines. Argersinger said investors might have to reassess how quickly UPS can recover from that blow and an overall sluggish economy, given the new development.
What it means for GXO: Lou Whiteman is curious to learn more about Amazon's plan but says the GXO sell-off appears overdone. "At least for now, it looks like the two companies are chasing different markets."
5. Shopify Can't Do Enough

Shopify (NASDAQ: SHOP) grew operating income by 88% and revenue by 34% in its most recent quarter, but management guided for slower growth up ahead. Shopify expects revenue growth only in the high-20% range in the current quarter. Investors are focused on the future, sending Shopify shares down 13.4% for the week.

No red flags: Shopify is hardly a sputtering business. For the first time, gross merchandise volume topped $100 billion in a single quarter, and operating income nearly doubled to $382 million. Shopify is growing more profitable with scale, a trend that can continue over time.
"It's clear that investors were expecting a more upbeat outlook." Matt Frankel blamed the stock sell-off on underwhelming operating profit guidance. The company does have a history of under-promising and over-delivering, but investors clearly wanted more.
6. Your Turn: High Conviction or High Risk?

When you see a high-quality "rule breaker" like The Trade Desk hit a multiyear low, is your first instinct to check your thesis and add to your position, or does the Publicis drama give you pause? Let us know your thoughts in the comments section below.

Tom Gardner has positions in Shopify and The Trade Desk. The Motley Fool has positions in Advanced Micro Devices, Amazon, Nvidia, Shopify, The Trade Desk, United Parcel Service, and eBay. The Motley Fool has a disclosure policy.
